title: A Completeness Theorem for Pseudo-Linear Functions with Applications to UC Security
speaker: Charanjit Jutla
affiliation: IBM T. J. Watson Research Center
abstract: We prove completeness results for certain class of functions which have implications for automatic proving of universally-composable security theorems for ideal and real functionalities composed of if-then-else programs with (uniform) random number generation and data objects from additive group of GF(2^m) .

proof_stages.label: UC realization by environmental indistinguishability
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up UC security as a real/ideal-world simulation definition in which an environment supplies inputs, observes outputs, and distinguishes executions, making the simulator rather than rewinding the central proof object.

proof_stages.label: Compositional reduction to simulator computation
proof_stages.summary: The UC composition theorem and hybrid-model viewpoint reduce protocol proof generation to deciding whether a serialized real-world behavior can be computed by a simulator from the outputs of ideal functionalities.

proof_stages.label: Pseudo-linear programming fragment
proof_stages.summary: To make this decision problem automatable, the speaker restricts protocols and ideal functionalities to finite branching programs with random generation, XOR-linear expressions, equality guards, and fixed-size tables, uniformly in the security parameter.

proof_stages.label: Monolithic pseudo-linear completeness theorem
proof_stages.summary: Pseudo-linear functions are defined as guarded linear expressions over large characteristic-two fields, and the main monolithic theorem states that any pseudo-linear target which is a function of given pseudo-linear inputs is already pseudo-linearly expressible from them.

proof_stages.label: Basis and interpolatability criterion
proof_stages.summary: Zero/nonzero guard-signature tables lead to elementary and then basic pseudo-linear polynomials, yielding an interpolatability test requiring rowwise linear dependence and identical coefficients on isomorphic rows.

proof_stages.label: Non-interpolatability implies non-functionality
proof_stages.summary: The key converse lemma asserts that if the interpolatability criterion fails, then over a sufficiently large field there are two points with the same values of all input functions but different target values, so the target is not even an arbitrary function of the inputs.

proof_stages.label: Iterated, randomized, and stateful simulations
proof_stages.summary: The monolithic completeness result is extended toward real simulator strategies that make iterated calls to ideal functionalities, where larger field bounds, randomized pseudo-linear choices, superguards, and eventually persistent variables handle the new degeneracies.

examples.name: Two-variable rowwise-linearity failure
examples.note: The functions involving x1, x2, and a guarded x1+x2 show that if the target is not a linear combination of the inputs on a guard-signature row, then it cannot be recovered from them, motivating the first interpolatability condition.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker builds the zero/nonzero row table for the two-variable functions and uses a rowwise linear-combination failure to conclude non-functionality

passes.reason: the two-variable pseudo-linear functions show failure of rowwise linear combination and hence non-computability from the inputs

examples.name: Brute-force password oracle
examples.note: An ideal functionality returning x only when queried with y=x shows that arbitrary iterated computation can recover x by exhaustive search, thereby forcing the theorem to exclude security-parameter-dependent brute-force simulations.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker defines the password oracle, runs the brute-force iteration over field values, and explains why it computes the password but is not pseudo-linear

passes.reason: the oracle collapses to zero except on the equality guard and refutes a naive unrestricted pseudo-linear iterated simulation claim
the oracle collapses all wrong guesses to zero and yields only a brute-force non-pseudo-linear iteration
the equality password oracle collapses to zero off the correct guess and refutes a naive unbounded implication to pseudo-linear simulation
the equality password oracle gives exhaustive-search computability without a pseudo-linear simulator for an unrestricted formulation
point password oracle is collapsed to zero off one value and minimally supported, showing an unrestricted brute-force dependence need not be pseudo-linear
the exact-match oracle is zero except on the password hit and shows unrestricted brute-force iterated computation need not be pseudo-linear
The oracle returning the password only on the exact guess is a collapsed sparse oracle used to show brute-force computability need not be pseudo-linear.
The singleton-hit password oracle gives brute-force computability but no pseudo-linear iterated simulation, a degenerate oracle edge case.
The password oracle is a collapsed point-test oracle and is used to show arbitrary iterated computation can recover the password only by non-pseudo-linear brute force.
singleton-success password oracle collapses off the correct guess and refutes naive pseudo-linear closure under arbitrary iteration
The password-check oracle collapses all wrong guesses to zero and shows unrestricted iterated computability need not be pseudo-linear.
the password-check oracle is a point-test functionality, used to show arbitrary iterated computation by brute force need not be pseudo-linear

examples.name: Reverse password oracle requiring randomness
examples.note: The reversed functionality, which reveals x except on the correct password, shows why deterministic pseudo-linear simulation is insufficient and motivates allowing probabilistic simulator programs.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker defines the reverse oracle and reasons that deterministic pseudo-linear simulation can fail while random sampling yields a probabilistic simulation
